questions.question:
The maximum gate delay for any output to appear in an array multiplier for multiplying two n-bit numbers is:
A. O(n^2)
B. O(n)
C. O(log(n))
D. O(1)
questions.to_find: Maximum gate delay for any output in an n-bit array multiplier

understand.plain: An array multiplier multiplies two n-bit numbers using an array of AND gates and adders. The question asks how many gate delays it takes before the final output is ready in the worst case. We need to trace the longest path through the circuit.

keywords.explain: A hardware circuit that multiplies two binary numbers using an array of AND gates for partial products and a series of adder rows to sum them.
keywords.example: To multiply two 4-bit numbers, the array has 4 rows of AND gates and 3 rows of adders.

keywords.explain: The time it takes for a signal to propagate through one logic gate. Total delay is the maximum number of gates on any path from input to output.
keywords.example: A 3-level AND-OR circuit has 2 gate delays.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='90' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='55' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>AND row 1</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='90' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='55' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Adder row 1</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='90' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='55' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Adder row 2</text><rect x='10' y='160' width='90' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='55' y='182' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Adder row n-1</text><line x1='55' y1='45' x2='55' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='55' y1='95' x2='55' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='55' y1='145' x2='55' y2='160' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='120' y='100' class='sub mut'>n-1 adder rows</text><text x='120' y='120' class='sub mut'>=> O(n) delay</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array multiplier structure: one AND row for partial products, followed by n-1 adder rows chained sequentially, giving O(n) total delay along the critical path.

given.aim: Find the maximum (worst-case) gate delay for the output of an n-bit array multiplier.

terms.meaning: Number of bits in each of the two operands being multiplied.
terms.connects: Determines the number of rows in the array and thus the critical path length.

terms.meaning: n rows of n AND gates each, computing all a_i * b_j products.
terms.example: For n=4: 4 rows of 4 AND gates = 16 AND gates total.
terms.connects: Each AND gate contributes 1 unit delay; these are the first level.

terms.meaning: n-1 rows of full adders that sum the partial products row by row.
terms.connects: Each row adds one gate delay; the carry chain through n-1 rows dominates.

given.plan: The critical path in an array multiplier goes through the AND gate (1 delay) and then through n-1 adder stages (each adding a constant number of delays). The total is proportional to n, giving O(n) delay.

steps.title: Identify the structure of the array multiplier
steps.formula_raw: Partial products: n rows x n AND gates
steps.apply: To multiply two n-bit numbers A and B, we first compute n partial products: PP_i = A AND b_i (shifted left by i). Each partial product row uses a single AND gate level — delay = 1.
steps.note: The AND gates are all in parallel, so the partial product generation takes only 1 gate delay regardless of n.

steps.title: Count the adder stages on the critical path
steps.apply: After partial products, there are n-1 adder rows stacked vertically. The carry must propagate through each row sequentially. Each row of full adders contributes a constant number of gate delays (say k). So total delay = 1 + k*(n-1) = O(n).
steps.note: This is the critical path: it runs vertically through all n-1 adder rows plus the initial AND stage.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Since the delay grows linearly with n (one per adder row, and there are n-1 rows), the maximum gate delay is O(n).
steps.note: O(n^2) would be the area (number of gates), not the delay. O(1) or O(log n) would require a different architecture (e.g., Wallace tree).

solution.result: The maximum gate delay for any output in an n-bit array multiplier is O(n).

questions.formula_note: Array multiplier critical path: 1 AND-gate level + (n-1) adder levels = O(n) gate delay. Area = O(n^2) but depth = O(n).

questions.question:
Consider an array multiplier for multiplying two n-bit numbers. If each gate in the circuit has a unit delay, the total delay of the multiplier is:
A. Theta(1)
B. Theta(log n)
C. Theta(n)
D. Theta(n^2)
questions.to_find: Total delay (in terms of unit gate delays) of an n-bit array multiplier

understand.plain: An array multiplier has a fixed structure: one level of AND gates for partial products and n-1 rows of adders. With unit delays for every gate, the total delay is the length of the longest path from input to output. Each adder row adds a constant delay, and there are n-1 rows, making the total Theta(n).

keywords.explain: A simplifying assumption where every gate (AND, OR, NOT, FA) has exactly delay 1. Total circuit delay = maximum number of gates on any path.
keywords.example: A chain of 5 gates has delay 5.

keywords.explain: Tight asymptotic bound — the delay grows exactly proportionally to n, i.e., it is both O(n) and Omega(n).
keywords.example: Theta(n) means if n doubles, the delay roughly doubles.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='180' viewBox='0 0 400 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='10' width='120' height='30' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='80' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>AND gates (delay 1)</text><rect x='20' y='55' width='120' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='80' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Adder row 1 (delay c)</text><rect x='20' y='100' width='120' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='80' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Adder row 2 (delay c)</text><rect x='20' y='145' width='120' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='80' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>... row n-1 (delay c)</text><line x1='80' y1='40' x2='80' y2='55' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='80' y1='85' x2='80' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='80' y1='130' x2='80' y2='145' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='180' y='90' class='lbl ink'>Total = 1 + (n-1)*c</text><text x='180' y='115' class='sub mut'>= Theta(n)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram of array multiplier delay: one AND gate level at top, followed by n-1 adder rows chained vertically. Total delay = 1 + (n-1)*c = Theta(n).

given.aim: Determine the exact asymptotic delay (Theta notation) of an n-bit array multiplier with unit gate delays.

terms.connects: Controls how many rows the array has: 1 AND row + (n-1) adder rows.

terms.meaning: The longest path from any input to any output in the combinational circuit.
terms.example: In a 4-bit array multiplier, the critical path goes through the AND stage and all 3 adder rows.
terms.connects: The total delay equals the length of the critical path.

terms.meaning: In unit-delay model, a full adder (implemented with gates) has a constant delay c.
terms.example: If a full adder uses 2 levels of gates, it has delay 2 in unit model.
terms.connects: Multiplied by (n-1) rows gives the dominant Theta(n) term.

given.plan: Count the number of gate layers on the critical path: 1 layer for AND gates + (n-1)*constant for adder rows = Theta(n). This is tight (lower bound is also Omega(n)) so the answer is Theta(n).

steps.title: Partial product generation delay
steps.apply: All n partial products PP_i = A AND b_i are computed in parallel using AND gates. In the unit delay model, each AND gate has delay 1. Since they are in parallel, the AND stage contributes exactly 1 unit of delay to the critical path.
steps.note: Even though there are n*n AND gates total, they all operate simultaneously — no sequential dependency.

steps.title: Adder rows delay
steps.formula_raw: delay_adders = (n-1) * c, where c is constant delay per adder row
steps.apply: There are n-1 adder rows. Each row adds the current partial product to the accumulated sum. The carry and sum outputs of row i feed into row i+1 — they are sequential. With unit delays, each full adder row has constant delay c. Total adder delay = c*(n-1).
steps.note: Even within a single adder row, there is a ripple carry — but for asymptotic analysis, each row contributes a constant (O(1)) number of gate levels.

steps.title: Compute total delay
steps.apply: Total = 1 + c*(n-1). As n grows, this is proportional to n. Both upper and lower bounds are linear: Omega(n) because the n-1 adder stages must be traversed, and O(n) because each stage is constant. Therefore, total delay = Theta(n).
steps.note: Theta(n^2) would be area complexity (total number of gates), not delay. O(log n) delay is achievable only with faster architectures like Carry-Lookahead or Wallace tree.

solution.result: The total delay of an n-bit array multiplier with unit gate delays is Theta(n).

questions.formula_note: Array multiplier depth = O(n): AND level (depth 1) followed by n-1 sequential adder rows (each constant depth), giving Theta(n) total delay.

questions.question:
The total number of Boolean functions which can be realised with four variables is:
A. 4
B. 17
C. 256
D. 65,536
questions.to_find: Total number of distinct Boolean functions of 4 variables

understand.plain: A Boolean function of n variables maps each combination of n input bits to either 0 or 1. With n variables there are 2^n input combinations, and for each we can independently choose 0 or 1 as output. The total number of distinct functions is 2^(2^n).

keywords.explain: A function f: {0,1}^n -> {0,1} that takes n binary inputs and produces a single binary output.
keywords.example: AND, OR, XOR are Boolean functions of 2 variables.

keywords.explain: A table listing all 2^n input combinations and the corresponding output for a Boolean function.
keywords.example: A 2-variable truth table has 4 rows; each distinct assignment of outputs is a distinct function.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='360' height='160' viewBox='0 0 360 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='340' height='50' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='180' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>n = 4 variables</text><text x='180' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Input combinations = 2^4 = 16</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='340' height='50' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='180' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Each combination: output = 0 or 1</text><text x='180' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Number of functions = 2^16 = 65,536</text><text x='180' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Formula: 2^(2^n) = 2^(2^4) = 2^16 = 65,536</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: With 4 variables, there are 2^4 = 16 input rows in the truth table. Each row can independently output 0 or 1, giving 2^16 = 65,536 distinct Boolean functions.

given.aim: Count the total number of distinct Boolean functions of 4 binary variables.

terms.example: Variables A, B, C, D each take value 0 or 1.
terms.connects: Determines the number of rows in the truth table.

terms.meaning: Total number of distinct input combinations for 4 variables.
terms.connects: This is the number of positions in the truth table output column.

terms.meaning: Number of ways to assign 0 or 1 to each of the 2^n positions.
terms.example: For n=2: 2^(2^2) = 2^4 = 16 Boolean functions of 2 variables.
terms.connects: This is the formula for counting all Boolean functions.

given.plan: Use the formula: the number of distinct Boolean functions of n variables = 2^(2^n). Substitute n=4 to get 2^(2^4) = 2^16 = 65,536.

steps.title: Count the number of input combinations
steps.apply: With n=4 binary variables, the total number of distinct input tuples is 2^4 = 16. These correspond to the 16 rows of a 4-variable truth table.
steps.note: Every Boolean function maps each of these 16 input tuples to 0 or 1.

steps.title: Count the number of distinct Boolean functions
steps.apply: For each of the 16 rows in the truth table, the output can independently be 0 or 1. The number of ways to fill the output column is 2^16 = 65,536. Each distinct filling represents a distinct Boolean function.
steps.note: This is the same as counting all 16-bit binary strings: 2^16 = 65,536.

steps.title: Verify
steps.note: For n=1: 2^(2^1) = 4 functions (F=0, F=1, F=A, F=A'). For n=2: 2^4=16 functions. For n=4: 2^16=65,536.

solution.result: The total number of Boolean functions of 4 variables is 2^(2^4) = 2^16 = 65,536.

questions.formula_note: Number of Boolean functions of n variables = 2^(2^n). For n=4: 2^16 = 65,536.

questions.question:
The Boolean expression A*D + A'*B is equivalent to:
A. A*D + A'*B
B. A'*B + A*B'
C. A XOR B
D. A'
questions.to_find: Which standard expression is equivalent to A*D + A'*B (where the expression involves complementation and operations between A, B)

understand.plain: From the image, the expression shown is A'*B + A*B̄ which is the XOR of A and B expressed as a sum of products. The question asks which of the given options is equivalent to the Boolean expression shown. The expression A'*B + A*B' is the standard SOP form of A XOR B.

keywords.explain: A Boolean expression written as an OR of AND terms (product terms), e.g., A'B + AB'.
keywords.example: A'B + AB' is the SOP form of A XOR B.

keywords.explain: Two Boolean expressions are equivalent if they produce the same output for all input combinations.
keywords.example: A'B + AB' and A XOR B are equivalent.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='380' height='140' viewBox='0 0 380 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='30' class='lbl ink'>A B | A'B | AB' | A'B+AB'</text><line x1='10' y1='38' x2='370' y2='38' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='20' y='60' class='sub ink'>0  0 |  0  |  0  |    0</text><text x='20' y='80' class='sub ink'>0  1 |  1  |  0  |    1</text><text x='20' y='100' class='sub ink'>1  0 |  0  |  1  |    1</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>1  1 |  0  |  0  |    0</text><text x='250' y='80' class='lbl ink'>= A XOR B</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Truth table for A'B + AB': outputs are 0,1,1,0 for AB = 00,01,10,11 — identical to XOR.

given.aim: Identify the expression equivalent to the given Boolean expression involving A and B.

terms.meaning: A is 0 AND B is 1 — the minterm where A=0, B=1.
terms.connects: One of the two minterms of XOR.

terms.meaning: A is 1 AND B is 0 — the minterm where A=1, B=0.
Output is 1 when the two inputs differ.
terms.connects: A'B + AB' = A XOR B — this is the standard SOP expansion of XOR.

given.plan: Recognize that the SOP form A'B + AB' has minterms at (A=0,B=1) and (A=1,B=0), which matches the XOR function. Verify using a truth table.

steps.title: Identify the minterms
steps.apply: The expression A'B + AB' has two product terms. A'B is true when A=0,B=1. AB' is true when A=1,B=0. So f=1 exactly when A and B differ.
steps.note: These are precisely the two minterms m1 and m2 in two-variable Boolean algebra.

steps.title: Build truth table and identify function
steps.apply: A=0,B=0: 1*0 + 0*1 = 0. A=0,B=1: 1*1 + 0*0 = 1. A=1,B=0: 0*0 + 1*1 = 1. A=1,B=1: 0*1 + 1*0 = 0. Output pattern: 0,1,1,0 — this is XOR. The equivalent SOP expression is A'B + AB'.
steps.note: This is the canonical expansion: every XOR gate can be built with an XNOR complemented, or directly from this SOP.

solution.result: The Boolean expression is equivalent to A'*B + A*B' (the SOP form of A XOR B).

questions.formula_note: A XOR B = A'B + AB' — this is the standard Sum-of-Products expansion of the exclusive-OR function.

questions.question: Let * be defined as a Boolean operation given as x * y = x'y + xy', and let C = A * B. If C = 1, then prove that A = B'.
questions.answer: A = B' (proof: C = A*B = A'B + AB' = 1 implies A != B, and under single-variable analysis A XOR B = 1 => A = NOT B)
questions.to_find: Prove that if C = A * B = 1 (where * denotes XOR), then A = B'

understand.plain: The custom Boolean operation * is defined as x*y = x'y + xy', which is exactly the XOR operation. If C = A*B = 1, we need to show that A must equal B'. Since XOR outputs 1 only when the two inputs differ, A XOR B = 1 means A != B, which in Boolean algebra means A = B'.

keywords.explain: Defined as x*y = x'y + xy', which equals x XOR y — output is 1 when inputs differ.
In Boolean algebra with only two values {0,1}, if A != B then A = B' (the complement of B).
keywords.example: If B=1 then B'=0, so A=0=B'.

understand.visual_alt: Proof table: A*B = A'B + AB' = 1 only when (A=0,B=1) or (A=1,B=0). In both cases, A = B' (complement of B).

given.aim: Prove that C = A * B = 1 implies A = B', given * is the XOR operation.

terms.meaning: x*y = x'y + xy' — this is XOR.
terms.connects: Knowing * = XOR lets us analyze when the output is 1.

terms.meaning: The XOR of A and B is 1.
terms.connects: Restricts the possible values of A and B.

terms.example: If B=0, then B'=1. If B=1, then B'=0.
terms.connects: We need to show A equals this value whenever C=1.

given.plan: Enumerate the cases where A*B = 1, and in each case verify that A = B'. Since Boolean variables only take values 0 or 1, this is a finite case analysis.

steps.title: Identify when A * B = 1
steps.apply: A'B = 1 requires A=0 AND B=1. AB' = 1 requires A=1 AND B=0. So A*B = 1 in exactly two cases: (A=0, B=1) or (A=1, B=0).
steps.note: These are the only input combinations that make XOR output 1.

steps.title: Check each case
steps.apply: Case 1: A=0, B=1. Then B'=1'=0=A. So A=B'. Case 2: A=1, B=0. Then B'=0'=1=A. So A=B'. In both cases, A=B'.
steps.note: Since these are the only two cases where C=1, and in both A=B', the proof is complete.

steps.title: Conclude the proof
steps.apply: The XOR operation outputs 1 if and only if the inputs are different. Since Boolean variables only take values in {0,1}, A != B is equivalent to A = B'. Therefore C = A*B = 1 => A = B'.
steps.note: Algebraically: A*B = A'B + AB' = 1. Multiply out: (A+B)(A'+B') = 1 — not helpful directly. The case analysis is the clearest proof.

solution.result: Proved: If C = A * B = 1 (where * denotes x'y + xy'), then A = B'. The XOR is 1 only when inputs differ, and in Boolean algebra differing inputs means one is the complement of the other.

questions.formula_note: XOR identity: A XOR B = 1 if and only if A != B, which in {0,1} means A = B' (complement). Used Boolean algebra case analysis.

questions.question: A switching function is said to be neutral if the number of input combinations for which its value is 1 is equal to the number of input combinations for which its value is 0. Compute the number of neutral switching functions of n variables (for a given n).
questions.to_find: Number of neutral Boolean (switching) functions of n variables

understand.plain: A switching function of n variables has a truth table with 2^n rows. The function is 'neutral' if exactly half the rows have output 1 and half have output 0 — i.e., exactly 2^(n-1) ones and 2^(n-1) zeros. We need to count how many such balanced functions exist.

keywords.explain: A Boolean function of n variables where exactly half of the 2^n input combinations give output 1 and half give output 0.
keywords.example: For n=2 (4 rows): exactly 2 ones. Functions like AB' + A'B (XOR) are neutral.

keywords.explain: The number of ways to choose k items from m items, C(m,k) = m! / (k! * (m-k)!).
keywords.example: C(4,2) = 6 — six ways to place 2 ones in 4 output positions.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='380' height='150' viewBox='0 0 380 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='360' height='40' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='190' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Truth table: 2^n rows</text><text x='190' y='48' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Need: exactly 2^(n-1) ones, 2^(n-1) zeros</text><rect x='10' y='65' width='360' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='190' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Choose which 2^(n-1) rows get output = 1</text><rect x='10' y='115' width='360' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='190' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Answer = C(2^n, 2^(n-1))</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Truth table with 2^n rows. A neutral function has exactly 2^(n-1) ones. Count = choose which 2^(n-1) rows get output 1 = C(2^n, 2^(n-1)).

given.aim: Count the number of neutral Boolean functions of n variables.

terms.meaning: Total number of rows in the truth table of a function of n variables.
terms.connects: Each neutral function must assign 1 to exactly 2^(n-1) of these rows.

terms.meaning: Half of 2^n — the required number of 1-outputs for a neutral function.
terms.connects: We need to choose which 2^(n-1) rows among the 2^n rows will have output 1.

terms.meaning: Binomial coefficient: number of ways to choose 2^(n-1) rows (to assign output 1) from 2^n total rows.
terms.example: n=2: C(4,2)=6 neutral functions out of 16 total.
terms.connects: This directly counts the neutral functions.

given.plan: A neutral function is fully determined by which 2^(n-1) rows of the truth table have output 1. We choose those rows from the 2^n available rows. The number of such choices is C(2^n, 2^(n-1)).

steps.title: Set up the counting problem
steps.formula_raw: Total rows = 2^n; ones required = 2^(n-1); zeros required = 2^(n-1)
steps.apply: A function of n variables has a truth table with 2^n rows. For the function to be neutral, the output column must have exactly equal numbers of 0s and 1s, i.e., exactly 2^n / 2 = 2^(n-1) ones.
steps.note: Neutrality requires n >= 1 (so 2^n is even, which it always is for n >= 1).

steps.title: Count neutral functions as combinations
steps.apply: Each neutral function corresponds to a unique selection of 2^(n-1) rows (from the 2^n rows) to assign output 1. The remaining 2^(n-1) rows automatically get output 0. The number of ways to make this selection is C(2^n, 2^(n-1)).
steps.note: For n=1: C(2,1) = 2 neutral functions: f=A and f=A'. For n=2: C(4,2) = 6 neutral functions.

steps.title: Verify with small example
steps.apply: For n=2, the 6 neutral functions are: AB, AB', A'B, A'B', A XOR B, A XNOR B (i.e., XOR'). Each has exactly 2 ones out of 4 rows. The formula C(4,2)=6 is confirmed.
steps.note: Total Boolean functions of n=2 variables = 2^4 = 16; neutral ones = 6, which is about 37.5%.

solution.result: The number of neutral switching functions of n variables is C(2^n, 2^(n-1)).

questions.formula_note: Combinatorics: number of n-variable Boolean functions with exactly k ones = C(2^n, k). For neutral functions k = 2^(n-1), giving C(2^n, 2^(n-1)).
